Vietnam fuel shortage persists -- imports 70% of jet fuel, 60%+ from Thailand and China (export bans still in effect, no lifting yet)
Thai AirAsia frequency cuts to 13 destinations remain in place -- pattern not yet reversed
April 13 is Songkran festival start (Apr 13-15) -- DMK will be heavily congested

Brent crashed 13% from $113-117 to ~$95.80 (April 8) -- biggest one-day drop since 1991 Gulf War
Short-haul route (2hr, 956km) -- limited fuel exposure per flight

SEA airspace completely unaffected by Middle East closures
April 13 departure gives 7-day buffer before Vietnamese visa expiry (April 20)

Ceasefire is a 2-week framework -- expires ~April 21, less than a week before BKK-CPH departure
EASA advisory expires April 10 -- renewal still likely while NOTAMs remain in place
Rerouting still adds 90 min to 3 hours via southern or northern corridor
Strait of Hormuz reopening contingent on Iran compliance -- physical oil normalization will take weeks

If ceasefire collapses, airspace risk reverts to HIGH within days

Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz as part of framework
Brent dropped 13% to ~$95.80 -- largest single-day fall since 1991 Gulf War
Thai Airways is government-backed flag carrier -- most resilient on this route
BKK-CPH still operating with rerouting -- no cancellation reported
Bangkok confirmed April 13-27: two Airbnb bookings in place

fuelOil.summary: Brent crashed from $113-117 intraday to ~$95.80 on April 8 (-13% to -15%) -- biggest one-day drop since 1991 Gulf War -- after Trump announced US-Iran 2-week ceasefire framework April 7 evening, Pakistan brokered. Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz. IATA jet fuel monitor still at $195/bbl (lags spot). Physical supply normalization will take weeks; backwardation expected to ease.
details: Brent crude futures at ~$95.80/bbl on April 8 (oilpriceapi.com), down from $113-117 intraday April 7-8
Drop of ~13-15% in single session -- biggest one-day Brent fall since 1991 Gulf War (NBC News, CNBC)
Trigger: Trump announced US-Iran 2-week ceasefire framework April 7 evening, Pakistan brokered (CNBC, NBC, Axios)
Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz as condition of ceasefire
IATA global jet fuel average: $195.19/bbl (April 6 reading -- monitor lags spot by days)
Spot/physical jet fuel still elevated due to supply chain lag; expect downward correction over coming week
US retail jet fuel: $4.88/gallon (~$205/bbl) as of early April -- pre-ceasefire reading
Pre-war IATA projection was $88/bbl for 2026 -- post-drop level $95.80 close to baseline
AirAsia X fare/surcharge hikes (announced April 6) not yet rolled back
China and Thailand fuel export bans still in effect -- no lifting announced as of April 8
Vietnam fuel delivery timelines still extended; 50+ day backlog persists
If ceasefire holds 2 weeks, IATA monitor likely shows $150-170/bbl by mid-April

geopolitical.iranConflict: MAJOR DE-ESCALATION April 7. Trump announced 2-week US-Iran ceasefire framework, Pakistan brokered. Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz. Brent crashed 13-15% on the news. Framework is provisional -- 2-week duration, not permanent. Airspace NOTAMs and EASA advisories not yet lifted as of April 8 morning.
airspaceClosures: Iran (OIIX FIR) -- still CLOSED to commercial aviation as of April 8
Israel (LLLL FIR) -- still RESTRICTED, limited PPR only
Bahrain, UAE, Qatar -- still HEAVILY RESTRICTED
EASA advisory expires April 10 -- renewal still likely until ceasefire is verified to hold
geopolitical.regionalStability: Southeast Asia airspace stable and unaffected by direct conflict. Regional fuel export bans (Thailand, China) still in effect April 8 -- no lifting announced. Vietnam supply chain still constrained. Ceasefire impact on physical SEA fuel availability will take 1-2 weeks to materialize.
details: Trump announced 2-week US-Iran ceasefire framework April 7 evening (NBC, CNBC, Axios, CNN)
Ceasefire brokered by Pakistan -- diplomatic surprise after April 5 escalation rhetoric
Iran agreed to allow safe passage through Strait of Hormuz
Oil markets reacted with biggest one-day drop since 1991 Gulf War
Framework is provisional 2-week negotiation window, not a permanent deal
Airspace NOTAMs in Iran/Iraq/Kuwait/Syria not yet lifted as of April 8 morning
EASA advisory expires April 10 -- still 2 days away; renewal call likely tied to NOTAM status
Strait of Hormuz physical normalization will lag the announcement by weeks
Risk reverts to HIGH if ceasefire collapses within the 2-week window (~April 21)
Kuwait International Airport drone strike was April 1 -- no new strikes reported since

airasia.cancellationRate: Elevated but stable. Thai AirAsia frequency cuts to 13 destinations still in place. AirAsia X 10% flight cuts still active. CXR-DMK FD647 still operating daily.
airasia.financialHealth: AirAsia X no fuel hedging. Q1 affiliate losses exceeded $180M. April 6 measures (30-40% fare hikes, 20% fuel surcharge, 10% network cut) not yet rolled back. Brent drop to $95 will ease cost pressure if it holds, but recovery will lag.
airasia.recentDisruptions: Thai AirAsia still operating CXR-DMK daily (FD647) as of April 8. Thai AirAsia frequency cuts to 13 destinations still in place. AirAsia X 10% network cut still in effect. Philippines AirAsia Manila-HCMC suspended through May.

thaiAirways.cancellationRate: Near normal for rerouted operations. BKK-CPH still operating with rerouting. Individual route cancellations still being reported.
thaiAirways.financialHealth: Exited bankruptcy June 2025 as privatized public company. Government-backed. 10-15% ticket price increases still in place. Brent drop to $95 will ease pressure if it holds.
thaiAirways.recentDisruptions: BKK-CPH operating with rerouting via southern or northern corridor. Adds 90 min-3hrs flight time. No BKK-CPH cancellations reported. Rerouting expected to remain in place until airspace NOTAMs lifted -- which will lag the ceasefire announcement.

cxrToDmk.notes: SEA airspace unaffected by Middle East closures. Primary risk: Vietnam jet fuel shortage and AirAsia operational reliability at 5 days out. FD647 still operating but verify in AirAsia app today (April 8). Songkran (April 13-15) starts on flight day -- DMK at peak congestion. Backup: VietJet or Vietnam Airlines CXR-BKK same day.

bkkToCph.notes: Traditional Gulf corridor still unavailable as of April 8. Thai Airways still rerouting via southern (Egypt-Saudi-Oman, +90min) or northern (Caucasus/Central Asia, +2-3hrs) corridors. EASA advisory expires April 10 -- renewal still likely while NOTAMs remain in place. Ceasefire announcement (April 7) should ease the trajectory but airspace reopening will lag by days/weeks. Expect 13-15hr flight instead of 11-12hr.

actions.action: Open AirAsia app today and verify FD647 CXR-DMK April 13 status. AirAsia X 10% network cut from April 6 not yet rolled back -- confirm FD647 not affected. If any disruption signal: book VietJet or Vietnam Airlines CXR-BKK same day immediately. 5 days out is the last viable backup window.

Track ceasefire status daily -- ceasefire is a 2-week framework expiring ~April 21, less than a week before BKK-CPH departure. If ceasefire collapses, BKK-CPH risk reverts to HIGH within 24h. Watch CNBC, Axios, Reuters daily for ceasefire updates.

Check safeairspace.net or ops.group on April 10 for EASA advisory status. Renewal still likely while NOTAMs in place. If lifted or narrowed: BKK-CPH rerouting may end early. If extended: rerouting confirmed through April 27.

Confirm Thai Airways BKK-CPH April 27 booking is active and has no schedule changes. Check thaiairways.com booking reference. Note: still expect 90min-3hrs longer than booked time unless airspace reopens before then.

oilPriceTimeline.event: Pre-conflict baseline. Markets calm, IATA forecast $88/bbl for 2026.
Brent drops to $67.4 as US-Iran diplomatic talks ease tensions.
Markets turn bullish anticipating US military action against Iran. Brent trading ~$10 above fair value.
Last day before strikes. Oil at pre-war levels.
US-Israeli strikes on Iranian military targets. War begins. Immediate price spike.
Iranian missile/drone retaliation. Brent surges 10-13% from pre-war.
Iranian drones strike Nakhchivan airport (Azerbaijan). Brent up 15% since war began.
Thailand bans fuel exports. China restricts refined oil exports.
Peak spike. Near halt in Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic. 65% increase from Feb 27.
Partial correction. Still up 37% from pre-war. IATA warns of sudden price challenge.
IATA weekly monitor: jet fuel $197/bbl for week ending Mar 20, up 58.4% from Feb 20.
Brent eases but jet fuel stays elevated due to supply chain lag.
Iran rejects US peace talks. Prices reignite. Jet fuel up 12.6% week-on-week.
Brent closes at highest since July 2022, up 4.2% on day.
Brent at 4-year highs. Jet fuel peaks at $234.3/bbl. Vietnamese airline cuts take effect April 1. Goldman Sachs: $14-18 geopolitical risk premium.
SHARP REVERSAL. Trump says war could end in 2-3 weeks. Brent drops $12/bbl (-10.3%). Spot Brent hits $141.36 -- highest since 2008. Kuwait airport struck by Iranian drones.
DE-ESCALATION FAILS TO HOLD. Brent reverses from $101 back to $112.42 (+11.2%). Trump signal not sustained. Physical supply crisis remains acute. IATA jet fuel avg: $195.19 (down 0.9%).
Slight easing. Brent dips to $109.77. AirAsia X declares 'real crisis', raises fares 30-40%, cuts 10% of flights. Trump escalates rhetoric.

MAJOR DE-ESCALATION. Trump announced 2-week US-Iran ceasefire framework April 7 evening (Pakistan brokered). Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz. Brent crashed 13-15% from $113-117 intraday to ~$95.80 -- biggest one-day drop since 1991 Gulf War. IATA monitor still at $195 (lags spot). Airspace NOTAMs not yet lifted.

cancellationTimeline.details: Normal disruption levels across SEA. Typical daily: 50-100 delays, <20 cancellations region-wide.
1,198 delays + 21 cancellations across Changi, Jakarta, Bali, Manila, Cebu. Pre-war disruptions.
4,216 delays + 62 cancellations. AirAsia alone had 247 delays. Infrastructure strain at SEA hubs.
Disruptions across Thailand, Japan, Indonesia, Vietnam, Philippines, Hong Kong.
1,400+ delays + 93 cancellations. Jakarta: 104 delays, KL: 470 delays.
WAR BEGINS. 2,513 cancellations + 2,117 delays. Changi: 32 cancelled. BKK: 32 cancelled. Massive wave.
150-300% above normal daily disruption rates. JAL, Thai Airways, Air India, ANA, AirAsia, Lion Air all affected.
328 Thailand flights disrupted. Hundreds of passengers stranded across Asia.
1,043 delays + 184 cancellations across SEA. AirAsia among hardest-hit carriers.
AeroThai reports cumulative 1,000+ flights cancelled in Thailand alone due to Gulf crisis.
65+ international/regional flights cancelled across Indonesia, Malaysia, Hong Kong, Taiwan.
325 delays + 50 cancellations in Thailand. Thai Airways, Bangkok Airways, AirAsia, Emirates affected.
Vietnamese airlines announce coordinated pullback from April 1.
Vietnamese airline cuts NOW ACTIVE. 5,993 delays + 278 cancellations across 19 Asian airports. Kuwait airport drone strike.
Thai AirAsia frequency cuts to 13 destinations confirmed. AirAsia X cancelled daily DMK-Shanghai Pudong. SAS cancelled 1,000 European flights.
AirAsia: 116 group delays. AirAsia X officially cuts 10% of flights, fares up 30-40% (April 6). Thai AirAsia still operating CXR-DMK (FD647).

Ceasefire announcement April 7 evening has not yet translated to operational changes. AirAsia X 10% cuts and Thai AirAsia frequency cuts still in place. CXR-DMK FD647 still operating. Operational normalization will lag the ceasefire by 1-2 weeks if it holds.

seaAirlineSummary: MAJOR de-escalation April 7: Trump announced 2-week US-Iran ceasefire framework (Pakistan brokered). Brent crashed 13-15% to ~$95.80 -- biggest one-day drop since 1991 Gulf War. Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz. Operational picture has not yet shifted: AirAsia X 10% cuts and 30-40% fare hikes still in place, Thai AirAsia frequency cuts still active, airspace NOTAMs not yet lifted. Recovery will lag the announcement by 1-2 weeks. Ceasefire is a 2-week framework, not a permanent deal -- expires ~April 21, less than a week before BKK-CPH.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Vietnam Airlines
seaAirlineOverview.impact: April 1 cuts active: 7 domestic routes suspended, 23 weekly flights cut. Ceasefire too recent to drive restoration.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Imports 70% of jet fuel, 60%+ from Thailand and China, both still maintaining export bans. Fuel delivery timelines still extended at 50+ days.

seaAirlineOverview.name: VietJet Air
seaAirlineOverview.impact: 8% regional network reduction active since April 1. Korea-Vietnam routes cancelled including Nha Trang flights.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Budget model highly vulnerable. Brent crash to $95.80 will ease pressure if it holds 2 weeks.

seaAirlineOverview.name: AirAsia (Group)
seaAirlineOverview.impact: AirAsia X: 10% flight cuts, fares up 30-40%, fuel surcharge up 20% (April 6) -- not yet rolled back. Thai AirAsia frequency cuts to 13 destinations still active.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: No fuel hedging. Thai AirAsia still operating CXR-DMK daily (FD647) as of April 8. Recovery from April 6 measures will lag ceasefire.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Thai Airways
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Rerouting European services via southern or northern corridor. BKK-CPH still operating. Raised prices 10-15%.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Exited bankruptcy June 2025. Government-backed but in recovery phase. Brent crash will ease pressure if it holds.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Cebu Pacific
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Major route suspensions: Davao-BKK, Iloilo-BKK, Iloilo-SGN, Clark-HAN all suspended Apr-Oct 2026.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Philippines fuel emergency still in effect.

seaAirlineOverview.name: SAS
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Cancelled 1,000 flights in April 2026 citing fuel crisis. Focusing on short-haul routes within Norway.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: European carriers responding aggressively. Relevant context for CPH arrivals.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Bangkok Airways
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Fuel surcharges of 1,200-2,000 baht. No major route suspensions reported.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Boutique positioning helps retain premium passengers.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Nok Air
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Fuel surcharges in effect. Domestic Thai routes under pressure.
seaAirlineOverview.detail: Low-cost model. Operating on thin margins.

seaAirlineOverview.name: Lion Air
seaAirlineOverview.impact: Part of broader disruption pattern. Indonesian domestic market under fuel pressure.

broaderContext.narrative: April 8 update: MAJOR de-escalation event. Trump announced US-Iran 2-week ceasefire framework April 7 evening (Pakistan brokered). Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz. Brent crashed 13-15% to ~$95.80 -- biggest one-day drop since 1991 Gulf War. This is the first credible diplomatic break since the Feb 28 strikes. Caveats: ceasefire is a 2-week framework (not permanent), expires ~April 21 -- less than a week before BKK-CPH. Operational recovery will lag the announcement: airspace NOTAMs not yet lifted, AirAsia X 10% cuts not rolled back, fuel export bans still in place. Risk picture has shifted from 'persistently high stress' to 'elevated but trending down -- contingent on ceasefire holding'. Watch the next 5-7 days for whether the framework sticks.

policies.description: PM Order 2/2026: Banned exports of gasoline, gasohol, diesel, Jet A-1 aviation fuel, and LPG (except to Cambodia and Laos). Still in effect.
Banned refined fuel exports, including jet fuel. Still in effect.
Declared fuel emergency. 4-day government work week.
Coordinated airline pullback in effect. Vietnam Airlines: 7 domestic routes suspended. VietJet: 8% regional reduction.
Military junta introduced odds-and-evens driving system (fuel rationing).
Trump announced 2-week US-Iran ceasefire framework, Pakistan brokered. Iran agreed to safe passage through Strait of Hormuz. Provisional negotiation window, not a permanent deal.

broaderContext.outlook: Elevated but trending down -- contingent on ceasefire holding. Key watch dates: April 8-21 (ceasefire framework window -- collapse reverts everything to HIGH within 24h), April 10 (EASA advisory renewal call), April 13 (AirAsia CXR-DMK + Songkran peak -- 5 days out, verify booking today), April 21 (ceasefire expiry -- less than a week before BKK-CPH), April 27 (Thai Airways BKK-CPH). If ceasefire holds and is extended, expect IATA jet fuel monitor to drop to $150-170/bbl by mid-April and airspace reopening to begin within 1-2 weeks.
